title: Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system causes linear memory growth (~500MB → 4GB+/day)
problem: Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 instances experience steady, usage-correlated memory growth from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over a full workday with 200 active users. Page loads degrade to 10+ seconds and API requests time out, requiring daily restarts. The issue is a regression introduced in the v2.2 → v2.3 upgrade.
root_cause_hypothesis: The 'improved real-time notifications' feature introduced in v2.3 is allocating per-request resources (likely event listener registrations, notification queue entries, or subscriber records) that are never released. This is not a connection leak — active TCP connections and file descriptors are normal — but rather an internal data structure that grows with every API request. At ~200 users × ~25 requests/user/hour, approximately 5,000 objects/hour accumulate without cleanup, producing the observed linear memory climb.
reproduction_steps: Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 on a server with monitoring (Grafana or equivalent)
Start the server and note baseline memory (~500MB)
Simulate normal usage with ~200 concurrent users performing standard task CRUD and commenting
Observe memory growth over 6-8 hours — expect ~300-400MB/hour growth under full load
Verify TCP connection count remains proportional to active users (not accumulating)
Compare with TaskFlow v2.2 under identical load — expect flat memory usage
environment: Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2), Go runtime, ~200 active users on weekdays. Server details not specified but memory behavior is consistent and reproducible day-over-day per Grafana dashboards.
impact: All 200 users on the instance experience progressively degrading performance throughout the day. By late afternoon, the application is effectively unusable (10+ second page loads, API timeouts). Requires daily manual restarts as a workaround. Any self-hosted v2.3 deployment with moderate user counts is likely affected.
recommended_fix: 1. Diff the v2.3 real-time notification code against v2.2 and identify per-request allocations — look for event registrations, subscriber maps, notification queues, or goroutine spawns tied to HTTP request handling that lack corresponding cleanup/deregistration. 2. Take a pprof heap profile (`go tool pprof http://host:port/debug/pprof/heap`) early in the day and again after several hours — compare to identify which object types are accumulating. 3. Ensure all per-request notification resources are released when the request completes (e.g., deregister listeners, close channels, remove map entries). 4. Consider whether a config flag like `TASKFLOW_REALTIME_ENABLED=false` should be exposed to allow operators to disable the feature as a mitigation.
proposed_test_case: Start a TaskFlow v2.3 instance, simulate 200 concurrent users making 25 requests/hour each for 8 hours, and assert that heap allocations (measured via pprof) remain bounded — total memory should not exceed baseline + a reasonable per-connection overhead (e.g., stay under 1GB). Compare against the same test on v2.2 as a regression baseline.
information_gaps: Exact pprof heap profile data showing which object types are accumulating (developer can gather this)
Whether a TASKFLOW_REALTIME_ENABLED config flag exists to disable the feature for confirmation (reporter is investigating but developer can check source)
Whether the leak is in the notification publisher side, subscriber side, or both
